title: Why Writing Your Book Alone Is Holding You Back: The Power of Book Midwives
guestName: Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski
guestBio: Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski are the co-founders of Synergy Publishing Group and creators of the Rebel Writers program. These 'book midwives' combine decades of writing expertise with a revolutionary approach to helping mission-driven women birth their books through community, embodiment, and retreat experiences.
excerpt: What if the secret to finally writing your book isn't more discipline or better time management, but community and embodied writing? Aimee sits down with her own 'book midwives' to reveal why women write differently and how to honor your unique creative process.
metaDescription: Discover why writing your book alone is holding you back. Learn the embodied approach to authorship that's changing how women write and publish their stories.
keyTakeaways: Writing is an embodied act that requires community, nourishment, and permission to be messy.
The defining question for any manuscript is: 'What absolutely has to be in this book?'
Women write differently than men and need to honor their unique creative processes rather than forcing traditional methods.
Your body knows your story before your mind does, so listen to what wants to emerge.
There's never a perfect time to write a book, just like having a baby or getting a puppy.
Legacy authors don't just write one book—they step into writing as part of their identity.
The people you least expect will be most transformed by your words, so don't hold back your story.

pullQuotes.text: What absolutely has to be in this book? That question changed everything for my writing process.
Your body knows your story before your mind does—we just need to create space to listen.
Writing doesn't happen in a vacuum. You need community, feedback, and care to birth something meaningful.

timestamps.label: Why Writing is an Embodied Act

content:
If you've been telling yourself that you just need more discipline to write your book, more time blocked on your calendar, or a better writing app, I'm about to challenge everything you think you know about the writing process. Because what if the best way to write your book is to never do it alone?

In this deeply personal episode, I sit down with Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski, the incredible women I call my book midwives. These are the founders of Synergy Publishing Group who didn't just help me write The Manage Her book—they completely transformed how I think about authorship, creativity, and the messy magic of bringing ideas into the world.

Writing as an Embodied Act

One of the most revolutionary concepts Shana and Cindy introduced me to is that writing isn't just a mental exercise—it's an embodied act. Your body holds your stories, your wisdom, your experiences in ways your logical mind hasn't even processed yet. This is why so many of us get stuck when we try to force our writing into rigid structures or follow someone else's blueprint for success.

Cindy explains it beautifully: your body knows your story before your mind does. We just need to create the space, the safety, and the community to let what wants to emerge actually come through. This is why their retreat experience at Synergy House includes yoga, nourishing meals, and spreading manuscript pages across every surface of their North Carolina property. Writing becomes a full-body, full-life experience rather than something you squeeze into the margins of an already overwhelming schedule.

This embodied approach completely changed my relationship with my own book. Instead of sitting at my computer trying to force words onto a blank page, I learned to trust the process, to let ideas flow in their own time, and to honor the way my creativity actually works rather than fighting against it.

The Question That Changes Everything

Here's the question that transformed my entire manuscript: What absolutely has to be in this book? Not what you think people want to hear, not what other business books include, not what feels safe or polished. What absolutely, without question, has to be in this book?

This question cuts through all the noise, all the second-guessing, all the imposter syndrome that keeps so many brilliant women from ever finishing their manuscripts. It forces you to get clear on your unique message, your specific wisdom, and the transformation you're here to create in the world.

For me, answering this question meant getting vulnerable in ways I hadn't expected. It meant including stories I wasn't sure I was ready to share, frameworks I was still developing, and truths that felt uncomfortably raw. But that's exactly what made the book authentic, powerful, and genuinely helpful to the women who needed to hear it.

How Women Write Differently

One of the most validating parts of working with Shana and Cindy was learning that women often write differently than men—and that's not a problem to solve, it's a strength to honor. Women tend to write in circles rather than straight lines, weaving stories together, making connections that might not seem logical at first but create deeper meaning and resonance.

We also tend to need more community, more processing time, more space to explore ideas out loud before committing them to paper. The traditional image of the solitary author locked away in a cabin might work for some people, but it doesn't serve most of the women they work with. Instead, their Rebel Writers program creates community around the writing process—women supporting women, sharing feedback, holding space for each other's creative emergence.

If you've been struggling with traditional writing advice, if you've started and stopped your book multiple times, if you need to talk through your ideas before writing them down, you're not broken. You're just honoring your own creative process, and it's time to find support systems that work with your nature rather than against it.

The Retreat Experience That Changes Everything

Synergy House isn't just a pretty location for writing retreats—it's a carefully crafted container for transformation. When women arrive, they're invited to spread their work across dining room tables, living room floors, and covered porches. They eat meals together, practice yoga together, and hold space for each other's creative breakthroughs and emotional releases.

This isn't about churning out chapters on a deadline. It's about creating the conditions where your best work can emerge naturally. Sometimes that means writing for hours in a flow state. Sometimes it means taking a walk to process a difficult chapter. Sometimes it means crying through a story you didn't know you needed to tell.

The retreat model recognizes that writing a book is often a spiritual and emotional journey as much as it is a practical one. You're not just organizing information—you're excavating wisdom, processing experiences, and stepping into a new identity as someone who has something important to say to the world.

Becoming a Legacy Author

Perhaps the most powerful shift Shana and Cindy offer is the concept of legacy authorship. This isn't about writing one book and checking it off your list. It's about stepping into writing as part of who you are, as a key way you contribute to the world and build the legacy you want to leave.

Legacy authors understand that their first book is just the beginning. They build creative partnerships, develop their voice over time, and see each project as part of a larger body of work that reflects their evolution as leaders, mothers, entrepreneurs, and women.

This long-term perspective takes so much pressure off any individual project. Your book doesn't have to be perfect or contain everything you'll ever want to say. It just needs to contain what wants to emerge right now, what absolutely has to be shared at this moment in your journey.

If you've been waiting for the perfect time to write your book, if you've been trying to force yourself through a process that doesn't fit how you naturally create, or if you've been wondering whether your story even matters, I want you to hear this: there's never a perfect time, your process is valid exactly as it is, and the people who most need your words are waiting for you to find the courage to share them.

The question isn't whether you have a book in you. The question is whether you're ready to find the community, support, and embodied approach that will help you birth it into the world.

quiz.title: What's Your Creative Expression Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally bring your ideas to life and what might be holding you back from sharing your story with the world.

questions.question: When you have a creative project you're excited about, what's your first instinct?

options.text: I need to feel into it first and let the idea marinate before I take action
I start researching the best methods and create a detailed plan
I immediately think about who I can brainstorm with or get feedback from
I envision the full impact this could have and start mapping out the bigger picture

questions.question: What tends to derail your creative momentum most often?

options.text: Trying to force myself to work when my body and energy aren't aligned
Getting stuck in planning mode and never feeling ready to start the messy work
Worrying that I don't have enough support or that I'm bothering people by asking for help
Getting overwhelmed by the scope of what I want to create

questions.question: When you think about sharing your story or expertise, what comes up for you?

options.text: I have so much to say but it doesn't always come out in a logical order
I need to organize my thoughts better before anyone would want to hear them
I wonder if anyone would actually be interested or if I'm qualified enough
I see how it could change everything, but the responsibility feels huge
Natural light, comfortable seating, and the freedom to move around or change locations
A dedicated workspace with all my tools organized and a clear schedule
Other people nearby, even if we're working on different things, or regular check-ins with collaborators
Inspiring visuals and reminders of the bigger mission driving this work

questions.question: What's your relationship with 'perfect timing' for big creative projects?

options.text: I believe in divine timing and trust that things will happen when they're meant to
I keep waiting for the right time when I have all the pieces in place
I'd feel more confident starting if I knew others were on the journey with me
I know there's never a perfect time, but I want to make sure the impact will be worth it

questions.question: When you imagine your creative work making an impact, what matters most to you?

options.text: That it feels authentic and true to who I really am
That it's well-crafted and provides real value to people
That it creates connection and helps people feel less alone
That it transforms how people think or creates lasting change

results.title: The Intuitive Creator
results.description: You trust your gut and know that your best ideas come when you're not forcing them. You understand that creativity is a full-body experience and you honor the messy, non-linear process of bringing something meaningful into the world. Your challenge is often finding the right community and structure to support your natural flow. This episode will show you how to embrace your embodied creative process and find the support system that honors how you actually work best.

results.title: The Structured Storyteller
results.description: You thrive with frameworks and clear processes, but sometimes wonder if you're missing something deeper in your creative work. You have incredible stories and wisdom to share, but you might be trying to force them into systems that don't quite fit. Your organized approach is a strength, but your stories deserve to breathe too. This episode will help you discover how to blend your love of structure with a more intuitive, embodied approach to creativity.

results.title: The Community Builder
results.description: You know instinctively that the best work happens when we're not alone, and you naturally create spaces for others to shine. You understand the power of collaboration and support, but you might hold back from fully stepping into your own story. Your gift for bringing people together is exactly what the world needs. This episode will inspire you to see how your community-focused nature can become the foundation for sharing your own powerful message.

results.title: The Legacy Visionary
results.description: You think big and see the long-term impact of the work you want to create in the world. You're not interested in one-time projects but in building something that will matter for years to come. Sometimes this big vision can feel overwhelming or paralyzing. Your desire to create lasting change is your superpower. This episode will show you how to step into your identity as someone who creates meaningful legacy work, starting with trusting what wants to emerge through you right now.
